many of them. Most of them were
friendly, but some were bad and
blOodthirsty, and a sort of block
house was maintained over on
Havermale island near where the
Great Northern depot is now for
the protection of the whites in case
of threatened massacre.


INDIANS HARD TO DEAL WITH.
Few of the Redskins could
speak the American language,
hence the merchants were com
PEELED to learn to speak Chinook.
In that lingo 'estes-ch' meant sugar
and semen was the name for to-
bacco; these and their fire water
and few other simple things were
about all they ever wanted. We
found the Indians were generally
honest and trustworthy, but it re-
uuired considerable diplomacy in
trading with them. For instance,
in weighing sugar for one of them
should the clerk let an overweight
of it into a sack and then begin to
remove some of it Mr.. Indian was
angry. He suspected that he was
being cheated and in many cases
would refuse to take the sugar.
FREDERICK POST HAD A
LITTLE FLOUR MILL.


Frederick Post had a two burr
flour mill on the present site of the
new Washington Water Power Co
building, just south of the Post
street bridge, at that time and


Post street was named in his honor.
"A. M. Cannon and a partner
named Warner had little sawmill
with a daily capacity of 1000 or 5000
feet of lumber, right where the
Sawmill Phoenix is now, and they
also conducted little store at the
northwest corner of Howard and
Front. Soon afterward Frederick &
Berg opened a new store. with a
general line of goods at the south
west corner of Main avenue and
Howard street, and these were the
only two general stores in the vii
lage of Spokane Falls.


s. HEATH, NOW CAPITALIST,


WAS A GROCERY CLERK.


IS. Heath, owner of the Heath
block and Heath's addition on the
north side, now one of the wealthy
men of the city, was a grocery clerk
in Cannon & Warmers store. Be.
fore the year of 70 was over, Spo-
kane was experiencing its first
promising building BOOM A man
named Goldsmith and F. r. Moore,


later an extensive owner in the
Wardner mines and a director of
the First National bank, built


what we thought then was very


fine store at the southeast corner OF


Front and Howard. on the present


site of the Occur d'Alene hotel. II


was a small affair, but it looked all
to the good.


SUPPLIES ALL BROUGHT
ACROSS THE PRAIRIE.


"All supplies for these trading
places were freighted in. They
were shipped up the Snake river
from the coast to Texas Ferry OF
Penewawa and sometimes in the
winter we had to freight the stuff
all the way over from Walla Walla
This made prices on some things
from the east pretty high, though
many common lines of foodstuffs
were about as cheap as now. We
didn't even have nickles. Bits
(12h cents) was the lowest price
quoted on anything, and the early
settlers expected nothing better


since many OF them had been In
